schools.summary: The confession of iniquity as the turn to mercy: 'if they shall confess their iniquity, and the iniquity of their fathers, with their trespass which they trespassed against me, and that also they have walked contrary unto me' — the confession — the great 'if' of mercy, after all the curses: if in exile they confess their iniquity and their fathers', owning their trespass and their walking contrary to God (the witnesses noting the significance — the turning point of the chapter, after the long descent of the curses, the door of mercy opening on confession, the honest owning of sin, the lesson — the way of restoration through confession and humbling, not a confession of words only but the broken acknowledgment of guilt, owning their own sin and their fathers', confessing that they had walked contrary to God and so deserved his contrary walking against them, the truth that God's mercy is reached through confession, 'if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins,' 1 John 1:9, 'I acknowledged my sin unto thee... and thou forgavest the iniquity of my sin,' Psalm 32:5, the example of Daniel's and Nehemiah's confession of the people's and the fathers' sins in exile, Daniel 9:5-8; Nehemiah 9:2, the figure — the confession of sin as the turn from judgment to mercy, the honest owning of guilt that opens the way to restoration, God waiting for the broken heart that confesses, the warning and the comfort that the way back is through confession); the confession of their own and their fathers' iniquity (the honest acknowledgment, Daniel 9:5-8) — the confession (the owning of guilt); 'with their trespass which they trespassed against me, and that also they have walked contrary unto me' (the owning of the trespass and the contrary walk, the broken acknowledgment, 1 John 1:9, the figure, the way of restoration through confession, the providence, the confession opening the door of mercy, vv.41-42) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The humbling of the uncircumcised heart: 'and that I also have walked contrary unto them, and have brought them into the land of their enemies; if then their uncircumcised hearts be humbled, and they then accept of the punishment of their iniquity' — the confession joined to the humbling of the heart and the accepting of the punishment as just (the I also have walked contrary unto them, God owning his contrary dealing, in answer to theirs, the if then their uncircumcised hearts be humbled, the proud, unyielding heart brought low, the heart circumcised by humbling, the accept of the punishment of their iniquity, owning the chastisement as deserved, submitting to it, the figure, the humbling of the proud heart, the uncircumcised heart, the heart of stubborn flesh, brought low and made tender, the accepting of the punishment as just the mark of true repentance, 'the sacrifices of God are a broken spirit: a broken and a contrite heart, O God, thou wilt not despise,' Psalm 51:17, the circumcision of the heart that God requires, Deuteronomy 10:16; Romans 2:29, the submission to God's just chastening); the proud heart brought low (the uncircumcised heart humbled, Deuteronomy 10:16) — the humbling (the heart made tender); 'accept of the punishment of their iniquity' (the owning of the chastisement as just, the submission, Psalm 51:17, the figure, the broken and contrite heart, the providence, the humbling, the covenant remembered, vv.42) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God remembering his covenant with the patriarchs: 'then will I remember my covenant with Jacob, and also my covenant with Isaac, and also my covenant with Abraham will I remember; and I will remember the land' — on their confession and humbling, God would remember his covenant with the patriarchs and the land (the remember my covenant with Jacob... Abraham, the covenant with the fathers recalled, the ground of mercy, the I will remember the land, the land not forgotten, the promise of restoration, the figure, God's remembering of the covenant the ground of restoration, the covenant with the fathers the anchor of hope, the truth that the covenant of grace, made with Abraham and confirmed to Isaac and Jacob, is the ground on which God restores his people, 'he hath remembered his covenant for ever,' Psalm 105:8, 'for the gifts and calling of God are without repentance,' Romans 11:29, the covenant remembered when the people return); the covenant with the patriarchs remembered (the ground of mercy, Psalm 105:8) — the remembrance (the covenant recalled); 'I will remember the land' (the land not forgotten, the promise of restoration, Romans 11:29, the figure, the covenant the anchor of hope, the providence, the covenant and land remembered, the restoration coming) — Calvin, Gill.

schools.summary: The land's sabbaths and the accepting of the punishment: 'the land also shall be left of them, and shall enjoy her sabbaths, while she lieth desolate without them: and they shall accept of the punishment of their iniquity: because, even because they despised my judgments, and because their soul abhorred my statutes' — the land would be left of them and enjoy its sabbaths in the desolation, while they accepted their punishment as just, because they had despised God's judgments and abhorred his statutes (the land... shall enjoy her sabbaths, the rest in the desolation, vv.34-35, the accept of the punishment of their iniquity, owning the chastisement as deserved, vv.41, the because they despised my judgments... abhorred my statutes, the cause, the contempt of vv.15, the figure, the land's rest and the people's submission joined, the just recompense owned, the despising of the law that brought the curse now acknowledged, the punishment accepted as deserved); the land's sabbaths in the desolation (the rest, vv.34-35) — the rest (the land's sabbaths); 'they shall accept of the punishment of their iniquity: because... they despised my judgments' (the punishment owned as just, the cause the contempt of the law, vv.15, the figure, the despising acknowledged, the chastisement accepted, the providence, the submission, the covenant faithfulness to follow, vv.44) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: God's covenant faithfulness that will not cast them away: 'and yet for all that, when they be in the land of their enemies, I will not cast them away, neither will I abhor them, to destroy them utterly, and to break my covenant with them: for I am the LORD their God' — the unbroken covenant — 'I will not cast them away, neither will I abhor them, to destroy them utterly, and to break my covenant with them': even in the land of their enemies, God would not utterly cast them off nor break his covenant (the witnesses noting the significance — the covenant faithfulness of God that survives all the judgment, the people chastened, scattered, almost consumed, yet not utterly cast away, the covenant not broken, the lesson — the unconquerable mercy and faithfulness of God to his covenant, though he chasten severely, he will not make a full end, will not break his covenant, will not utterly destroy, the remnant preserved, the covenant kept, the wider truth — the preservation of Israel and the faithfulness of God, 'I am the LORD, I change not; therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed,' Malachi 3:6, 'hath God cast away his people? God forbid... God hath not cast away his people which he foreknew,' Romans 11:1-2, the survival of the Jewish people through all the centuries a standing witness to this word, the figure — the faithfulness of God that holds his covenant fast even through the severest judgment, the mercy that chastens but will not destroy, the comfort that God's people are never utterly cast away, 'for I am the LORD their God,' the ground of all hope of restoration, that God remains their God even in their exile); God not utterly destroying them (the remnant preserved, Malachi 3:6) — the mercy (not a full end); 'to break my covenant with them: for I am the LORD their God' (the covenant not broken, God remaining their God, Romans 11:1-2, the faithfulness, the covenant kept, the providence, the people preserved, the ground of restoration) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: God remembering the covenant of the ancestors brought out of Egypt: 'but I will for their sakes remember the covenant of their ancestors, whom I brought forth out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the heathen, that I might be their God: I am the LORD' — God would remember the covenant made with the generation he brought out of Egypt before the nations, that he might be their God (the remember the covenant of their ancestors, the covenant at the exodus, with the fathers of the nation, the whom I brought forth out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the heathen, the redemption witnessed by the nations, the that I might be their God, the purpose of the redemption and covenant, the I am the LORD, the seal, the figure, the covenant of the redemption remembered, the exodus-covenant the ground of mercy, the truth that God's purpose in redeeming them, 'that I might be their God,' holds fast and grounds the restoration, the redemption witnessed by the nations a pledge that God will keep his covenant, his honor among the heathen bound up with his faithfulness, Ezekiel 20:9, 14); the covenant of the exodus generation remembered (the ground of mercy) — the remembrance (the covenant recalled); 'whom I brought forth out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the heathen, that I might be their God: I am the LORD' (the redemption witnessed by the nations, that God might be their God, his honor bound up with his faithfulness, Ezekiel 20:9, the figure, the redemption the pledge of the covenant, the providence, the covenant remembered, the chapter closing) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Gill.
